nativeRange.habitat: Silty, sandy, and rocky pools, runs, and sometimes riffles of creeks and small to medium rivers; tolerant of siltation, turbidity, reduced water quality, and seasonally intermittent flows

decorPreferences: smooth rocks and pebble substrate mimicking stream bed
fine-leaved plants or java moss for spawning crevices
driftwood or flat stones as shelter
powerhead or circulation pump to provide moderate current

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality flake, micro-pellets, and frozen or live foods including brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, and mosquito larvae. Also grazes on soft algae and vegetable matter; spirulina-based foods enhance male breeding coloration. Wild diet consists of aquatic and terrestrial insects and algae. Feed 2–3 small portions daily.

avoidWith: long-finned species (betta, fancy guppies, angels) — fin-nipping risk
very slow or sedentary fish that cannot compete at feeding
tiny dwarf shrimp fry (may be eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: Typical FishBase species illustration; verify availability and attribution before use.

summary: A hardy, unheated-tank minnow native to central North America, the Red Shiner rewards aquarists with electric breeding coloration — iridescent pink-purple-blue flanks and red fins on males. An excellent cool-water community fish, though long-finned tankmates should be avoided.
careNotes: The Red Shiner is a small, active schooling minnow ideally suited to unheated indoor aquariums in the 10–24°C range, making it an outstanding choice for temperate setups alongside White Cloud Mountain Minnows, zebra danios, and hillstream loaches. A group of at least 6 (ideally 10+) is necessary for natural schooling behaviour and to distribute any mild intraspecific chasing. The tank should be well-filtered and well-oxygenated; moderate water movement via a powerhead or spray bar replicates the flowing-creek conditions the species prefers. Hardness and pH are not demanding — neutral to slightly alkaline, moderately hard water (pH 7.0–7.5, dH 10–20) is ideal, consistent with FishBase and fishkeeper.co.uk data. The species is notably tolerant of lower temperatures and cooler seasonal fluctuations, and has been kept successfully in outdoor ponds in temperate climates over summer. Avoid housing with bettas, angelfish, or other long-finned fish as the Red Shiner is prone to fin-nipping. Family note: formerly placed in Cyprinidae, elevated to Leuciscidae by Tan & Armbruster (2018). Invasive concern: widely established outside its native Mississippi basin and Gulf drainage range through bait-bucket releases; do not release aquarium fish into natural waterways. Often sold as feeder fish but is an excellent display species.
breedingNotes: Crevice spawner; spawning season runs mid-April through September at temperatures around 22–26°C. Females release multiple egg clutches daily into rock crevices, gaps in decor, or among coarse substrate, averaging 400–600 eggs per clutch across many clutches per season. Young-of-year fish are capable of spawning, accelerating establishment in new environments. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days; fry become free-swimming after approximately one week. Parents do not guard eggs and will eat them — remove adults or provide dense moss cover. Feed fry infusoria or liquid fry food initially, transitioning to baby brine shrimp once free-swimming. Breeding males display spectacular iridescent pink-purple-blue flanks with a red crown and vivid red-orange fins.

primary.notes: Primary source. additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 6.3–7.6 cm, temperature 15–26°C, pH 6.5–8.0, kH 2–15, minimum 20-gal tank, schooling requirement (6–8+), omnivore diet, crevice-spawning breeding details, peaceful temperament, captive-bred availability, 3–5 year captive lifespan. Used to extend temperature upper bound to 26°C and confirm lifespan to 5 years in captivity.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Red Shiner profile
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 10–22°C, pH 7.0–7.5, hardness up to 20 dH, max 9 cm, schooling 6+, crevice spawning (400–600 eggs), 3–5 day hatch, mid-to-top swim zone, strong filtration and high oxygenation requirement, cool winter rest period recommendation, avoid long-finned tankmates. Also listed as 'Rainbow Dace'.

additional.name: NAS USGS — Red Shiner species profile
additional.notes: Nonindigenous Aquatic Species database. Confirmed invasive distribution (bait bucket introductions), ecological impact (predation on native fish eggs/larvae), native range extent, and commercial availability as bait and aquarium fish.

fish.breedingNotes: Crevice spawner; spawns readily at 22–26°C in mature tanks with rock gaps or coarse substrate. Eggs hatch 3–5 days; fry free-swimming after ~1 week. Parents must be removed to prevent egg predation. Multiple clutches per season; 400–600 eggs per clutch.
